extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property is VIOLATED. Compare two inputs differing only in dizziness, with back_pain=true, pallor=false, abdominal_pain=false.

Before (dizziness=false): Only r4 matches (back_pain -> urgent, priority 2). r2 requires both back_pain AND dizziness, so it does not fire. No higher-priority matching rule. Result = urgent.

After (dizziness=true): r2 now matches (back_pain AND dizziness -> self_care, priority 100) and r4 also matches (back_pain -> urgent, priority 2). Conflict resolution is by priority per output; r2's priority 100 beats r4's priority 2, so r2 wins. Result = self_care.

In enum order [self_care, routine, urgent, emergent], setting dizziness from false to true drops acuity from urgent down to self_care -- strictly lower. This is exactly the prohibited behavior. The root cause is that the highest-priority rule (r2) maps a dizziness-gated combination to the lowest acuity (self_care), so enabling dizziness can override the urgent rules.

The witness assignment is the "after" state (dizziness=true) that yields the lowered acuity self_care; the comparison baseline is the identical assignment with dizziness=false yielding urgent.
